---
author: "Equipo EDUMER"
bibliography: "../input/bib/merit-factorial.bib"
csl: "../input/bib/apa6.csl"
---

# Introduction


Despite rising economic inequality and limited social mobility in contemporary societies [@chancel_world_2025; @lopez-roldan_comparative_2021], meritocratic beliefs remain remarkably widespread among citizens [@mijs_paradox_2019; @sandel_tyranny_2020]. Meritocracy refers to the idea that social rewards are, and should be, allocated according to individual effort and ability rather than social origins or personal connections [@young_rise_1958]. This persistence is sociologically consequential because meritocratic beliefs shape how people interpret inequality and fairness in unequal societies [@madeira_primes_2019; @sandel_tyranny_2020]. Schools are especially important in this regard, as they constitute a central setting in which these beliefs are formed early in life [@resh_sense_2014; @mijs_unfulfillable_2016; @batruch_belief_2022]. Through grading, tracking, selection, and everyday evaluative practices, schools do not merely transmit abstract ideas about achievement; they make “merit” visible, consequential, and socially meaningful [@traini_stratification_2022; @darnon_where_2018; @batruch_belief_2022]. Understanding how young people perceive and endorse meritocratic beliefs is therefore crucial for tracing the emergence of judgments about fairness and inequality during early socialization.

Although the importance of this issue, empirical research on meritocratic beliefs remains conceptually and operationally fragmented. Survey-based studies have used a wide range of measures, ranging from broad attitudes toward inequality and social mobility to beliefs about individual responsibility, success, and structural constraints [@mccoy_priming_2007; @castillo_meritocracia_2019]. Besides, most studies rely on single-dimensional indicators and often conflate descriptive perceptions (how meritocracy works) with normative preferences (how it should work) [@liu_does_2025; @tang_meritocratic_2025]. A related problem concerns the treatment of non-meritocratic principles. Even when researchers distinguish merit factors from non-meritocratic ones (e.g., family wealth or connections), these dimensions are often collapsed into a single continuum through subtraction or reverse-coding [e.g., @reynolds_perceptions_2014]. This practice embeds a zero-sum assumption that is theoretically nontrivial and empirically risky: if merit and non-merit beliefs can coexist rather than functioning as strict opposites, then stronger recognition of non-meritocratic barriers may be misread as weaker endorsement of meritocracy [@zhu_meritocratic_2025]. To address these limitations, this paper adopts the multidimensional framework proposed by Castillo et al. [-@castillo_multidimensional_2023], which conceptualizes meritocratic beliefs as distinct but related dimensions by differentiating between perceptions and preferences, as well as between meritocratic and non-meritocratic principles. Although this framework has received empirical support in adult populations, it has not yet been tested among school-aged students in everyday school contexts.

The meaning of meritocracy is especially consequential in school settings, where it operates not only as a cultural ideal but also as a central organizing principle of institutional life. Schools promote meritocracy as a normative ideal by presenting education as a legitimate pathway to upward mobility and by framing individual effort and achievement as the proper basis of social reward [@vandewerfhorst_meritocracy_2024; @witteveen_reconsidering_2020]. Yet this promise coexists with persistent inequalities in later-life outcomes and with evidence that schooling often reproduces inequality through stratified and unequal learning environments [@bourdieu_reproduction_1990; @goudeau_hidden_2017a; @zhou_equalization_2019]. At the same time, students encounter meritocracy not only as discourse but also as a lived experience through grading, selection, and tracking, which make “merit” visible and consequential [@traini_stratification_2022; @darnon_where_2018; @resh_sense_2014; @tang_meritocratic_2025]. Research further shows that stronger endorsement of meritocratic beliefs in school is associated with greater justification of inequality [@batruch_belief_2022; @wiederkehr_belief_2015; @lampert_meritocratic_2013; @tang_meritocratic_2025; @mijs_unfulfillable_2016], even as students may simultaneously recognize structural constraints, reflecting a form of “dual consciousness” [@liu_does_2025]. Taken together, these tensions make schools a crucial setting for examining how adolescents understand meritocracy, non-meritocratic constraints, and the fairness of unequal outcomes.

Against this background, this paper examines whether school-aged students conceptualize meritocratic beliefs in multidimensional terms. Building on the framework proposed by Castillo et al. [-@castillo_multidimensional_2023], we extend this approach to adolescents in everyday school contexts. Specifically, we ask:

(1) Do school-aged students distinguish between meritocratic and non-meritocratic principles, as well as between perceptions (“what is”) and preferences (“what should be”)?

(2) Can these dimensions be measured in ways that are comparable across age cohorts and stable over time?

We argue that a multidimensional conception of meritocratic beliefs clarifies what adolescents actually endorse—descriptions, ideals, or both—and provides a stronger basis for valid developmental and longitudinal analysis. To examine this, we use novel survey data and test measurement invariance across two age cohorts (8th and 10th graders) and two waves (2023 and 2024), thereby assessing the stability and comparability of these dimensions across groups and over time.
